 {"id":"franken_networkx-c3m8","title":"parity: DiGraph mutation methods should preserve NetworkX edge insertion order","description":"DiGraph mutation methods currently disturb observable edge order beyond the base iteration drift. After starting from a->b, b->a, b->c, FrankenNetworkX places add_edge('c','d') and update(edges=[('c','d', ...)]) in different positions than NetworkX, and remove_edge('b','a') leaves the residual edge order mismatched. Match NetworkX edge insertion-order behavior across DiGraph add_edge, update(edges=...), and remove_edge.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T08:16:32.626017386Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-21T08:16:32.626017386Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"franken_networkx-hl4","title":"Add doc comments to all epsilon/threshold constants in fnx-algorithms","description":"## What

Add explanatory doc comments to all named constants at the top of
crates/fnx-algorithms/src/lib.rs (lines 15-27) explaining:
- What each constant controls
- Why this specific value was chosen
- What NetworkX does for comparison
- What happens if the value is too large or too small

## Current Constants

```rust
const PAGERANK_DEFAULT_ALPHA: f64 = 0.85;          // NX default is also 0.85
const PAGERANK_DEFAULT_MAX_ITERATIONS: usize = 100; // NX default is also 100
const PAGERANK_DEFAULT_TOLERANCE: f64 = 1.0e-6;     // NX default is also 1e-6
const KATZ_DEFAULT_ALPHA: f64 = 0.1;                // NX default is also 0.1
const KATZ_DEFAULT_BETA: f64 = 1.0;                 // NX default is also 1.0
const KATZ_DEFAULT_MAX_ITERATIONS: usize = 1000;    // NX default is also 1000
const KATZ_DEFAULT_TOLERANCE: f64 = 1.0e-6;         // NX default is also 1e-6
const HITS_DEFAULT_MAX_ITERATIONS: usize = 100;     // NX default is also 100
const HITS_DEFAULT_TOLERANCE: f64 = 1.0e-8;         // NX default is also 1e-8
const DISTANCE_COMPARISON_EPSILON: f64 = 1.0e-12;   // Our own threshold
const FLOW_EPSILON: f64 = 1.0e-10;                  // Our own threshold
const ASSORTATIVITY_EPSILON: f64 = 1.0e-15;         // Our own threshold
const PARAM_SUM_EPSILON: f64 = 1.0e-6;              // Same as NX
```

## Why This Matters

These constants define the numerical behavior of the entire algorithm library.
Without documentation, future contributors might:
- Change PAGERANK_DEFAULT_ALPHA without realizing it breaks NX parity
- Tighten FLOW_EPSILON to 1e-15 causing infinite loops in min_cost_flow
- Loosen DISTANCE_COMPARISON_EPSILON causing non-deterministic shortest paths

## Files To Change

- crates/fnx-algorithms/src/lib.rs (lines 15-27)

## Template

```rust
/// Damping factor for PageRank. NetworkX default: 0.85.
/// Higher values weight link structure more; lower values bias toward uniform.
/// Must match NX for parity — do not change without updating conformance fixtures.
const PAGERANK_DEFAULT_ALPHA: f64 = 0.85;
```","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:05:57.639838945Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-25T03:12:17.235104755Z","closed_at":"2026-03-25T03:12:17.234589159Z","close_reason":"Added comprehensive doc comments to all 13 constants in fnx-algorithms. Each documents: purpose, NX default match, what happens if changed, and why this specific value.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"franken_networkx-rbda","title":"DOCS: First-time user experience — quickstart, examples, API reference","description":"## Gap
README.md has a Quick Start section but the user experience for someone discovering the library is thin:
- No expanded API reference (just the algorithm table in README)
- No migration guide from NetworkX
- No performance comparison data visible to users
- No contributing guide
- No doctest verification that examples actually work

Note: PyPI publication (ciq6) and CI (zrlb) are now COMPLETE. Release workflow and E2E tests exist. This bead focuses on user-facing documentation.

## What to create

### 1. docs/ directory structure
- docs/quickstart.md: expanded tutorial with real-world graph analysis examples
  - Social network analysis (build graph, find communities, compute centrality)
  - Road network shortest paths (weighted Dijkstra)
  - Biological network (bipartite, clustering)
- docs/migration.md: 'switching from NetworkX' guide
  - Side-by-side code comparisons for top 20 patterns
  - What's identical, what differs, known limitations
  - Backend mode (zero changes) vs standalone mode
- docs/algorithms.md: algorithm reference with complexity and examples
  - Grouped by family (shortest path, centrality, community, etc.)
  - Each entry: signature, complexity, example, NX equivalent
- docs/performance.md: benchmark comparison vs NetworkX
  - Pagerank: 20x speedup chart
  - Shortest path: comparison data
  - Community detection: comparison data
  - Memory usage comparison
- docs/backend.md: NetworkX backend integration guide
  - Setup, configuration, which algorithms dispatch to Rust
  - Which algorithms still fall back to NX and why
- docs/contributing.md: developer guide
  - How to build (maturin develop)
  - How to run tests (pytest, cargo test)
  - Code structure (11 crates, Python wrapper)
  - How to add a new algorithm

### 2. Example scripts
- examples/basic_usage.py: standalone quickstart
- examples/backend_mode.py: using as NX backend
- examples/social_network.py: real-world analysis
- examples/benchmark_comparison.py: reproduce perf claims

### 3. PyPI metadata enhancement
- pyproject.toml: update long_description, classifiers, URLs
  - project-urls: Documentation (link to docs/), Repository (GitHub), Changelog
  - classifiers for Topic::Scientific, Status::Production, Framework::NetworkX

### 4. Doctest verification
- Create scripts/verify_docs.py that extracts code blocks from .md files and runs them
- Add to CI as a documentation gate

## Testing requirements
- Every code example in docs/ must be runnable and produce claimed output
- scripts/verify_docs.py: extract and run all Python code blocks from docs/*.md
- Verify all links in README/docs are valid (no dead links)
- Verify pyproject.toml metadata renders correctly on PyPI

## Files to create
- docs/quickstart.md, migration.md, algorithms.md, performance.md, backend.md, contributing.md
- examples/basic_usage.py, backend_mode.py, social_network.py, benchmark_comparison.py
- scripts/verify_docs.py: documentation verifier
- Update pyproject.toml metadata

## Acceptance criteria
- New user can follow quickstart to working code in < 5 minutes
- Migration guide covers top 20 NetworkX patterns with before/after
- All code examples verified runnable via scripts/verify_docs.py
- PyPI page shows rich metadata with description, links, classifiers","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-06T15:50:34.163105605Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-07T02:03:28.510857373Z","closed_at":"2026-04-07T02:03:28.510596129Z","close_reason":"Added first-time-user docs set, runnable examples, docs verifier, CI docs gate, and richer PyPI metadata","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

+    def test_undirected_all_shortest_paths_bellman_ford_parity(self, fnx, nx):
+        G_fnx = fnx.Graph()
+        G_nx = nx.Graph()
+        for graph in (G_fnx, G_nx):
+            graph.add_edge("a", "b", weight=1.0)
+            graph.add_edge("b", "c", weight=1.0)
+            graph.add_edge("a", "d", weight=1.0)
+            graph.add_edge("d", "c", weight=1.0)
+            graph.add_edge("c", "e", weight=1.0)
+            graph.add_edge("b", "e", weight=2.0)
+
+        _assert_same_result_or_exception(
+            lambda: list(
+                fnx.all_shortest_paths(
+                    G_fnx, "a", "e", weight="weight", method="bellman-ford"
+                )
+            ),
+            lambda: list(
+                nx.all_shortest_paths(
+                    G_nx, "a", "e", weight="weight", method="bellman-ford"
+                )
+            ),
+        )
+
